10.1016/j.jmp.2021.102600
Broadly speaking, there are two different views about how recognition memory is conceptualized. One view is based on a continuous memory representation for targets and foils on a memory strength (or alternatively strength-of-evidence) axis. This approach was initially proposed by  Egan (1958) , and it was a reapplication of signal-detection theory (SDT), originally developed for sensory detection ( Tanner & Swets, 1954 ). The second theoretical view about recognition memory is based on multinomial processing tree (MPT) models. All MPT models are measurement tools for a specific task. Unlike the signal-detection approach, MPT models are minimalistic theories that avoid stipulating a presumed psychological continuum with location and spread parameters, and these models instead focus on a probability-tree representation of the task. Examples of this class of memory representations include: the 6P and 7B models ( Chechile, 2004 ), the 2HT model ( Bröder & Schütz, 2009 ), and the IES model ( Chechile, Sloboda, & Chamberland, 2012 ). There are differences among these MPT models, but they share the idea that the memory representation can be characterized with a probability tree model that has a measure of detection (or sufficient target storage) and has one or more parameters to account for guessing.

The simplest of the above mentioned MPT models is the two-high threshold (2HT) model, which posits that there are only three states: (1) a target-detection state, (2) a foil-detection state, and (3) an uncertain state. 
                            1 
                         
                         
                            1 
                            Prior to the paper by  Bröder and Schütz (2009) , there were earlier memory models that also used two thresholds for recognition memory that resulted in three states (e.g.,  Atkinson & Juola, 1974; Snodgrass & Corwin, 1988 ). Yet the Atkinson–Juola model was designed to account for recognition search and decision; whereas the Snodgrass–Corwin model assumed that the probability for the target detection was the same as the probability for foil detection, which is not an assumption made in the Bröder–Schütz version of the 2HT model. It should also be pointed out that the one-high-threshold (1HT) model ( Swets, 1961 ) is an even more simple MPT memory model, but it can be considered a special case of the 2HT model where the probability of foil detection is zero. 
                          In the Bröder–Schütz version of the 2HT model, there are only two possible states for old items — they are either in the target-detection state or in the uncertain state. The parameter  
                            
                               
                                  D 
                               
                               
                                  o 
                               
                            
                          is the probability that the target is in the detected state. Targets in the detected state have a hit rate of 1, but targets in the uncertain state have a guessing hit rate of  
                            g 
                         . So, one of the two thresholds is associated with the detection of targets. Importantly a target is never detected as a foil, thus a miss is assumed to be an incorrect guess from a target in the uncertain state. Although one might conceptualize the detection of targets as being based on a latent target strength that is above the threshold value, this narrative is not necessary because the mathematical model only stipulates a hit rate of 1 when the target is detected and a guessing hit rate of  
                            g 
                          when it is not detected. Foils are also only in two possible states — either in a foil-detection state (with probability  
                            
                               
                                  D 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                         ) or in the uncertain state (with probability  
                            
                               1 
                               − 
                               
                                  
                                     D 
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                  
                               
                            
                         ). When foils are in the uncertain state, they are assumed to have the same guessing and rating probabilities as the target when it is in the uncertain state.  Bröder and Schütz (2009)  point out “Targets can never cross the ‘new’ threshold, whereas distractors can never cross the ‘old’ threshold. This is why both thresholds are referred to as ‘high’ thresholds”. (p. 588). However, these authors also state that “... it may be more natural to refer to the 2HTM as the  high-low-threshold model  (e.g.,  Yonelinas & Parks, 2007 ), but we stick to the term used in most of the literature”. (p. 588). However, the implicit language of an underlying evidence strength, where a target is either above or below a high value and the foil is either above or below a low threshold value, is not necessary because the mathematics of the 2HT model only has parameters for the discrete states and the common guessing rate (i.e., a latent strength narrative is not formally a part of the mathematical formalism). 
                            2 
                         
                         
                            2 
                            Although a latent strength might be hypothesized for the 2HT model as suggested by  Bröder and Schütz (2009) , other MPT theorists have vigorously rejected that concept. For example,  Chechile (2018)  argued that a memory encoding, such as hearing a few bars of a symphony, involves a rich stimulus pattern that cannot be reduced to a single strength value. He argued that memory differs from the sensory detection of a simple tone, which can vary in intensity but not in its tonal structure. SDT emerged from modeling the psychophysical detection of pure tones versus featureless noise. But  Stevens (1961)  noted a major difference in psychophysics between qualitative and quantitative stimuli. Quantitative stimuli, such as brightness, can be meaningfully ordered on a single dimension, but qualitative stimuli, such as hue,  cannot be ordered . Stevens thus argued that magnitude estimation and signal detection is appropriate for only quantitative stimuli.  Chechile (2018)  likewise argued that memory strength itself is a problematic way to conceptualize the memory of an event. This idea will arise later in the rationale for an alternative model to the 2HT model, which is discussed in Section  4.2 . 
                          Most importantly, the 2HT model, unlike the SDT model, does not require the identification of location and spread parameters for the distributions for targets and foils on an underlying strength continuum.

The 2HT model has been the center of considerable debate (e.g.,  Dube & Rotello, 2012; Dube, Starns, Rotello, & Ratcliff, 2012 ). Critics of the 2HT model have focused on the shape of the ROC curve. But the ROC curves for various theories have long been difficult to discriminate, even for evaluating alternatives to the SDT model in the field of sensory detection (see  Laming, 1973 ). For example it is challenging to ascertain that the curvilinear ROC of the SDT model is superior to the ROC for say the 7B model, which is a more complex MPT model than the 2HT model ( Chechile, 2014 ). Moreover model assumptions can be modified to justify differences in the ROC.  Kellen and Klauer (2015)  point out that a ROC comparison is “... a procedure that is predicated on substantial auxiliary assumptions, which if violated can invalidate results” (p., 542). They suggest that a more profitable method for model evaluation is to employ a critical test. We agree with this point. In so doing, we follow the approach by  Kellen and Klauer (2014)  using the multi-item ranking task. But the Bröder–Schütz version of the 2HT model is designed for an old/new recognition testing procedure where the probes are either targets or foils, so it is necessary to first describe the 2HT model in terms that are suitable for the ranking task.

Participants in the ranking task are presented with a lineup consisting of a single target along with  
                            
                               n 
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                          foils, and the participants are instructed to rank the items in the order of the likelihood that each is the target. However, we believe that the ranking task can also provide for a different critical test of the 2HT model that does not depend on an experimental manipulation of strength. This test is in terms of an analysis of the hazard function for ranking task data. The critical test does not require assumptions about the model parameters across different experimental conditions because it is based on the hazard function for any condition.

Hazard functions were first used in actuarial statistics ( Steffensen, 1930 ), but they have grown to be important in many disciplines, including mathematical psychology.  Townsend and Ashby (1978)  introduced hazard functions to mathematical psychology, and there are now a number of known properties of the hazard function that can be found in this literature ( Chechile, 2003, 2006; Luce, 1986; Townsend & Ashby, 1983 ).

Hazard functions have been important for studying the properties of stochastic processes, in part because they provide a sensitive way of detecting a misspecified theory. For example, rival theories for describing the response-time distribution can be difficult to discriminate in terms of their density functions, but they can have qualitatively different and distinguishable hazard functions (e.g.,  Van Zandt, 2002 ).

For a continuous probability distribution on a univariate ordered support set  
                            X 
                         , the hazard function  
                            
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x

                               x

                          does not explicitly appear in the  
                            
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  2 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          equation, it does not mean that  
                            
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  2 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          is the same for each condition because, as noted above, the  
                            
                               q 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  k 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          distribution can vary across experimental conditions. Nonetheless an examination of the hazard function for all values of  
                            k 
                          does lead to a critical test of the 2HT model. But before examining this issue, it is convenient to first discuss some basic theorems and ideas that are useful for hazard function analyses.

Historically the study of hazard functions has been based on mathematical analyses of the properties of a probability distribution (e.g.,  Barlow & Proschan, 1965; Chechile, 2003; Luce, 1986; Thomas, 1971 ). An important concept for this paper is what  Keilson and Gerber (1971)  call a  strictly log-concave  or SLC sequence. These authors explored the concept of  strong unimodality , which is defined as a distribution that has a mode and where the convolution of the distribution with another unimodal sequence also has a mode. Let  
                         
                            P 
                            
                               ( 
                               i 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       be a discrete function with support on the integers  
                         
                            i 
                            ∈ 
                            
                               ( 
                               L 
                               , 
                               L 
                               + 
                               1 
                               , 
                               … 
                               , 
                               M 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                      . They established that the condition of strong unimodality occurs if and only if  
                         
                            (4) 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           [ 
                                           P 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              k 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           ] 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  ≥ 
                                  P 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     k 
                                     + 
                                     1 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  
                                  P 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     k 
                                     − 
                                     1 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  
                                  , 
                               
                            
                         
                      for  
                         
                            k 
                            ∈ 
                            
                               ( 
                               L 
                               + 
                               1 
                               , 
                               … 
                               , 
                               M 
                               − 
                               1 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                      . A strictly log-concave or SLC sequence is the special case of a strict inequality for the strong unimodality condition.

Besides the importance of the linkage of unimodality to SLC sequences, there is also a connection between a SLC sequence and hazard. Keilson and Geber state that a SLC sequence has  monotonically increasing hazard , but they do not provide a formal proof for that proposition. 
                         6 
                      
                      
                         6 
                         
                            Chechile (2003)  proved that a discrete probability distribution that belongs to a form class called Pólya frequency functions of order two (P 
                               
                                  
                                     F 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                            ) has nondecreasing hazard.  Brenti (1988)  showed that a sequence that is a P 
                               
                                  
                                     F 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                             function implies that it is also log-concave. Thus the claim by  Keilson and Gerber (1971)  that a SLC sequence is a monotonically increasing hazard (MIH) sequence is a reasonable statement, but we have not been able to find a formal proof that a SLC sequence implies that it has monotonically increasing hazard. However, Keilson and Geber did cite a difficult to find book for this claim ( Karlin, 1968 ). 
                       Accordingly, we present a theorem proving that all SLC sequences have monotonically increasing hazard. The theorem will be more general than only for discrete probability distributions that have the SLC property because the theorem also applies for any discrete sequence regardless if it is a probability distribution or a  subprobability  sequence. There are some specialized applications that involve a distribution that is not a normalized probability function. The values in the distribution are real non-negative numbers, but the sum of all the values is less than  
                         1 
                      . The term some mathematicians use for such a sequence is a  subprobability distribution  ( Bertoin & LeJan, 1992; Graczyk, 1994; Rao & Shanbhag, 1991 ). Furthermore, given the definition of the hazard from  (2) , it does not depend on the distribution being a normalized probability function, so a subprobability sequence also has a hazard function. Moreover, if we were to multiply any probability distribution by a constant  
                         
                            
                               
                                  a 
                               
                               
                                  ∗ 
                               
                            
                            > 
                            0 
                         
                      , then from the definition of hazard from  (2)  there still would be hazard values even when  
                         
                            
                               
                                  a 
                               
                               
                                  ∗ 
                               
                            
                            > 
                            1 
                         
                      . Consequently, if all terms in a sequence are real, nonnegative values, then the sequence has a hazard function.

                            has monotonically increasing hazard.

                                  − 
                                  1 
                               
                             implies that  
                               
                                  b 
                                  q 
                                  > 
                                  a 
                                  r 
                               
                            . Moreover because  
                               
                                  P 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     i 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             is a SLC sequence, it follows that  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        r 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  > 
                                  q 
                                  s 
                               
                            . Thus it follows that  
                               
                                  b 
                                  q 
                                  
                                     
                                        r 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  > 
                                  a 
                                  r 
                                  q 
                                  s 
                               
                            , which in turn implies that  
                               
                                  b 
                                  r 
                                  > 
                                  a 
                                  s 
                               
                            . So, via mathematical induction we have established for  
                               
                                  j 
                                  ∈ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     0 
                                     , 
                                     … 
                                     , 
                                     M 
                                     − 
                                     k 
                                     − 
                                     1 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                             that

                                        =

                                                       k 
                                                       + 
                                                       j 
                                                       )

                                                       k

                                                       1

                                                       j

                                                       1 
                                                       )

                                        ≥ 
                                        0 
                                        .

                                                 k 
                                                 + 
                                                 j

                                                 k

                                                 1

                                                 j

                                                 1

                                        .

                                  > 
                                  0

                               0

                                  = 
                                  0 
                               
                            . Summing over

Corollary 3 
                         
                            If a discrete distribution is a SLC sequence of nonnegative real values, then it has a unique mode.

If a discrete distribution is a SLC sequence of nonnegative real values, then it has a unique mode.

Proof 
                         For a proof, see  Keilson and Gerber (1971) , theorem 3). They established that the necessary and sufficient condition for a unimodal distribution is that the distribution must be a SLC sequence (i.e.,  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           [ 
                                           P 
                                           
                                              ( 
                                              k 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           ] 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  > 
                                  P 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     k 
                                     − 
                                     1 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  P 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     k 
                                     + 
                                     1 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                            ). □

For a proof, see  Keilson and Gerber (1971) , theorem 3). They established that the necessary and sufficient condition for a unimodal distribution is that the distribution must be a SLC sequence (i.e.,  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           [ 
                                           P

                                              k 
                                              ) 
                                           
                                           ]

                                  > 
                                  P 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     k 
                                     − 
                                     1 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  P 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     k 
                                     + 
                                     1 
                                     ) 
                                  
                               
                            ). □

The next theorem will also be useful in the following sections. It states that if a sequence

                               ( 
                               k

                       is also SLC.

                       distribution as shown in  (3) . It is convenient to examine this stochastic process in terms of the number of foils in the uncertain state.  Fig. 2  illustrates the stochastic process from this perspective. If the target is in the target-detection state with probability  
                         t 
                      , then the target will be given the rank of 1; otherwise the target is in the uncertain state along with any foils that are also in the uncertain state. Let  
                         
                            Q 
                            
                               ( 
                               k 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       denote the probability that there are  
                         k 
                       items in the uncertain state, which consists of the target and  
                         
                            k 
                            − 
                            1 
                         
                       foils. Because items in the uncertain state are assumed to have the same guessing rate, it follows that each of the  
                         k 
                       possible arrangements for the target’s rank is equally probable. For example, if there were three items in the uncertain state, then there are three equally probable arrangements, which are TFF, FTF, and FFT. Thus the target in general has a  
                         
                            1 
                            / 
                            k 
                         
                       chance for each rank position from 1 to  
                         k 
                      . The remaining  
                         
                            n 
                            − 
                            k 
                         
                       foils are in a detected state (i.e., these items are known to be foils), so the probability is zero that the target is in rank positions  
                         
                            k

                            1 
                         
                       to

                       distribution needs to be specified. There are two different versions of the 2HT model corresponding to the  homogeneous  and  heterogeneous  forms for the  
                         
                            Q 
                            
                               ( 
                               k 
                               ) 
                            
                         
                       distribution.

For the homogeneous case, each of the  
                            
                               n 
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                          foils has the same chance of  
                            p 
                          for being in the uncertain state. Because the undetected target is also in the uncertain state, the probability that  
                            k 
                          items (the one target and  
                            
                               k

                               1 
                            
                          foils) are in the uncertain state is  
                            
                               Q 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  k 
                                  )

                               k 
                               −

                               n

                               k 
                            
                          foils are in the certain state of being known novel items. Thus the homogeneous case for the 2HT model reduces to  
                            
                               n 
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                          binomial trials where

                                  ( 
                                  k

                               k 
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                          uncertain-state outcomes and  
                            
                               n

                               k 
                            
                          certain-state outcomes. Thus

The homogeneous version of the 2HT model for the ranking task is based on five foundational assumptions which are: (1) the target is never in the foil-detection state, (2) foils are never in the target-detection state, (3) all items in the uncertain state have the same guessing rate, (4) all foils have the same chance  
                            p 
                          for being in the uncertain state, and (5) the target in the undetected state along with  
                            
                               k 
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                          foils has the same probability of  
                            
                               
                                  1 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                          for being in rank positions  
                            1 
                          to  
                            k 
                         . For the heterogeneous version of the 2HT model the fourth assumption is dropped. Thus, for the heterogeneous model, we let each foil have a potentially  distinct  probability,  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                         ,  
                            
                               i 
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  { 
                                  1 
                                  , 
                                  
                                  … 
                                  , 
                                  
                                  n 
                                  − 
                                  1 
                                  } 
                               
                            
                          for being in the uncertain state. Thus each foil can be regarded as an independent Bernoulli variable that has a value of either  
                            1 
                          (with probability  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                         ) if it is in the uncertain state or  
                            0 
                          (with probability  
                            
                               1 
                               − 
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                            
                         ) if it is detected as a foil. The sum of this collection of potentially different independent Bernoulli variates is called the  Poisson-binomial  distribution. The binomial distribution is a special case of the Poisson binomial where all Bernoulli variates are identical.

In general the Poisson binomial is a discrete probability distribution with values  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  i 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          on the support set of integers  
                            
                               i 
                               ∈ 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0 
                                  , 
                                  
                                  1 
                                  , 
                                  
                                  … 
                                  , 
                                  
                                  n 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          for some  
                            
                               n 
                               > 
                               1 
                            
                         . 
                            7 
                         
                         
                            7 
                            Note here for the discussion of the general properties of the Poisson-binomial distribution that  
                                  n 
                                is simply the number of Bernoulli variates; whereas later the distribution will be applied to the ranking task where the number of Bernoulli variates is  
                                  
                                     n 
                                     − 
                                     1 
                                  
                               . 
                          The sum of  
                            n 
                          non-identical Bernoulli variables is called the Poisson binomial because of the fact that as  
                            
                               n 
                               → 
                               ∞ 
                            
                          and for very small  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                         , where  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     ∑ 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                     = 
                                     1 
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               λ 
                            
                          stays constant, the distribution is approximated by the Poisson distribution ( Feller, 1957  p. 264). But  Johnson, Kotz, and Kemp (1992)  pointed out that the appellation  Poisson binomial  can be confusing because the exact general case differs from the asymptotic limiting approximation.  Aitken (1945)  called the distribution of the sum of Bernoulli indicators the  binomial distribution of Poisson . But in this paper we will continue to use the more common name Poisson-binomial distribution for the general finite case of a sum of  
                            n 
                          Bernoulli variables with probabilities  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                         . The mean and variance of this distribution are described in  Appendix .

The heterogeneous version of the 2HT model for the ranking task, based on Eqs.   (1) 
                               , 
                               (13) 
                               ,  
                               (14) and (15)  
                               and with  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           P 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                           − 
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        i 
                                        − 
                                        1 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               being the probability from a Poisson-binomial distribution, has monotonically increasing hazard for  
                               
                                  
                                     k 
                                     > 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               .

Proof 
                            When the ranking task has  
                                  
                                     n 
                                     = 
                                     2 
                                  
                                stimuli, (i.e., there is only one foil), the 2HT model is equal to the homogeneous model, so the theorem has increasing hazard as shown in  Theorem 3 . Consequently, the simplest case for the heterogeneous version of the 2HT model is when  
                                  
                                     n 
                                     = 
                                     3 
                                  
                               , and there are two foils. Let us begin an inductive proof by first examining the  
                                  
                                     n 
                                     = 
                                     3 
                                  
                                case. For this case, we need only consider  
                                  
                                     k

Four foundational assumptions were previously identified for the heterogeneous version of the 2HT model for the ranking task — one less assumption than for the homogeneous case. In this subsection we describe another memory threshold model that is similar to the heterogeneous model, but it is based on only three assumptions. The assumptions are: (1) a target that is detected with probability  
                            t 
                          receives a rank of  
                            1 
                         , and a non-detected target is in an uncertain state, (2) foils are never in the target detection state, and (3) all foils compete with a target in the uncertain state on the basis of their relative strength. Since this model features a competition of the target and foils in the uncertain state (or U state), it is called the U-state competition model. The second assumption is also made for both the homogeneous and heterogeneous versions of the 2HT model, but the first and third assumptions are different from the assumptions listed in the previous subsection. A key reason for the change is because the U-state competition model drops the idea that a foil can be detected as novel. In this alternative threshold model, there is a single threshold for the target. Like the other two versions of the 2HT model for the ranking task, a non-detected target is in an uncertain state with probability  
                            
                               1 
                               − 
                               t 
                            
                         , but unlike the previous two versions, the uncertain state contains all  
                            
                               n 
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                          foils. Consequently, if the target is in the uncertain state, there are  
                            n 
                          items in this state.

Another key difference from the previous versions of the 2HT model is that the items in the uncertain state are not all equivalent (i.e., guessing and confidence rating responses are not the same for all U-state items). Instead it is postulated that each item has a latent strength, so the relative ranking between a pair of U-state items is based on which member of the pair has the greater strength and thus receives the lower ranking. So, each foil has a Bernoulli-process probability value  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          for having a strength greater than the target. That is, it is a Poisson-binomial stochastic process for the  
                            
                               n 
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                          contests between the target and the foils. But suppose that  
                            b 
                          foils have a trivially small  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          value (e.g., say  
                            
                               . 
                               000000001 
                            
                         ), then these  
                            b 
                          foils have a vanishingly small chance of beating a target in the U state. For those  
                            b 
                          competitions, the target will almost certainly win and thus receive a lower rank value. Thus the feature of the 2HT model, where a subset of the foils is detected as novel, is approximated in the U-state competition model by some trivially low  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          values of a general Poisson-binomial process for the competition between the target and the foils. Thus the  
                            
                               q 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  k 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          values for the U-state competition model are obtained from a  
                            
                               n 
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                          Poisson-binomial distribution. Hence the ranking probabilities for the U-state competition model have the following probabilities:  
                            
                               (34) 
                               
                                  
                                     R 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        k 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     
                                        
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                    t 
                                                    + 
                                                    
                                                       ( 
                                                       1 
                                                       − 
                                                       t 
                                                       ) 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       
                                                          P 
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          n 
                                                          − 
                                                          1 
                                                       
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       ( 
                                                       0 
                                                       ) 
                                                    
                                                    , 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    k 
                                                    = 
                                                    1 
                                                 
                                              
                                              
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       ( 
                                                       1 
                                                       − 
                                                       t 
                                                       ) 
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       
                                                          P 
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          n 
                                                          − 
                                                          1 
                                                       
                                                    
                                                    
                                                       ( 
                                                       k 
                                                       − 
                                                       1 
                                                       ) 
                                                    
                                                    , 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    k 
                                                    > 
                                                    1 
                                                    , 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                            
                         where  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                     − 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  i 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          is the Poisson-binomial conditional probability for the outcome of  
                            i 
                          foils having a strength greater than the target. It is clear from  (34)  for  
                            
                               k 
                               > 
                               1 
                            
                          that the hazard for this model is equal to the hazard of the Poisson binomial.

Because the Poisson binomial is a general probability model that has applications outside of psychology, we frame the next theorem in terms of the distribution rather than for the U-state competition model.

Theorem 5 
                            
                               The Poisson-Binomial distribution is a SLC sequence, which has a unique mode, and the distribution has monotonically increasing hazard.

The Poisson-Binomial distribution is a SLC sequence, which has a unique mode, and the distribution has monotonically increasing hazard.

It follows from  Theorem 5  that the U-state competition model has monotonically increasing hazard.

The U-state competition model differs from the heterogeneous version of the 2HT model in several important ways because (1) all foils are in the uncertain state, and (2) the items in the uncertain state are not all equivalent. Yet this model can approximate the process in the 2HT model, where some foils are detected as novel, by a subset of the U-state competition probabilities having trivially small values; thereby making those foils virtually certain to have a rank position larger than the target. So, we include the U-state competition model as a modified version of the 2HT model for the ranking task. Importantly, this model also has monotonically increasing hazard for  
                            
                               k 
                               > 
                               1 
                            
                         . Thus the hazard is monotonically increasing for  
                            
                               k 
                               > 
                               1 
                            
                          for all three versions of the 2HT model for the ranking task. Since hazard can be measured, this prediction for the hazard function can be examined experimentally and thereby provide a critical test for the 2HT model. Yet for this test to be informative, it should be established that not all models of recognition memory necessarily predict that the hazard function is monotonically increasing. A discussion of this issue is explored in the next section.

If evidence were found that reliably established a local hazard function minimum for  
                         
                            k 
                            > 
                            2 
                         
                      , there would be strong evidence against the 2HT model. But it is important to ascertain if there are any models that would be consistent with a local hazard minimum for  
                         
                            k 
                            > 
                            2 
                         
                      . Two alternatives to the 2HT model will be examined in this section to see if they too predict a monotonically increasing hazard function for the ranking task.

Although the 2HT model for the ranking-lineup task predicts monotonically increasing hazard for  
                            
                               k 
                               > 
                               1 
                            
                         , the hazard at  
                            
                               k 
                               = 
                               1 
                            
                          can be larger than  
                            
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  2 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          because there is an additional increase caused by the targets being detected (i.e., the probability of  
                            t 
                          that the target is detected). But the signal detection model is not limited to any range for  
                            k 
                         .

To study the signal detection model for the ranking task, sampling simulations were examined by using R software.

                          The variety of hazard profiles exhibited in the table is sufficient to establish that the signal detection model does not predict only a monotonically increasing hazard function.

Although the  Kellen and Klauer (2014)  version of the 2HT model for the ranking task does not explicitly postulate an underlying strength for targets and foils, the similar high-low threshold model of  Yonelinas and Parks (2007)  for a standard recognition memory task does use a strength framework upon which there are two thresholds. Also a latent strength was used for the U-state competition model. But, as mentioned in Section  1.1 , there is a principled argument against the concept of memory strength. From an information-encoding prospective, the study of a target item involves the storing of a complex qualitative collection of perceptual, associative, and cognitive productions that cannot be represented by a single univariate number such as a strength value ( Chechile, 2018 ). For example, Chechile reported that one participant described what was encoded to the stimulus of a nonsense syllable LRH; the participant claimed he generated the sentence “Lynda rides horses” and created an image consistent with the sentence. In psychophysics,  Stevens (1961)  stressed the distinction between stimuli that are  prothetic  (or qualitative) and  metathetic  (or quantitative) and argued that only metathetic stimuli can be rank ordered. In a similar fashion, memory encodings of stimuli that are typically studied in experiments are qualitative and should not be represented by a single intensity value. Consequently the MPT models generated by Chechile and associates have explicitly stressed, in their narrative about the models, the idea that encoding involves information that can be either complete, fragmented, or sometimes missing altogether. The narrative intentionally rejects the concept of memory strength for encodings that have qualitative features. 
                            10 
                         
                         
                            10 
                            This argument against a strength representation is only relevant for stimuli that have qualitative features. If the stimulus is featureless and has only an intensity, then the memory representation can only be based on that intensity value. The SDT model for such stimuli would be appropriate. Furthermore, while the above argument is suitable to motivate an alternative measurement model that avoids the idea of strength, the argument itself is not scientific evidence. Others can reasonably argue that complex memory stimuli can be ordered in terms of the similarity of the foils to the target. Also this counterargument could say that people can impose a common dimension over a set of qualitative objects such as cars or flowers. One can further hypothesize that the testing procedure itself imposes an ordering. However, the counterargument in favor of a strength model is also not scientific evidence, but it does support the exploration of strength models for memory measurement. Ultimately evidence for any approach to memory measurement will depend on the relative predictive success of the various models, and it will depend on the degree of experimental validation for the models.

From an information prospective, the processes underlying recognition and recall should instead be based on the information that is still available at the time of test. The  Chechile and Soraci (1999)  model is an example of this approach. In that model, there are three states for the target representation at the time of test. One state is for sufficient target storage, where there is enough detail to support item recall, provided that the person can retrieve the encoding. The second state is one where the information remaining in memory is a fragment. While the fragment is insufficient for recalling the entire target, it can be enough to enable the person to reject some of the foils presented because those foils mismatch the stored fragment. The third state is when the encoding of the target event is entirely missing; in this case the person performs at a pure chance level. In this paper, we will provide a modified version of the Chechile–Soraci MPT model. The original Chechile–Soraci model was designed for the task where there was an initial recall test that was followed by a series of forced-choice recognition tests. The modified model, without the initial recall test, is designed for the  
                            n 
                         -item ranking task. Henceforth let us call this the CS model.

Fig. 3  provides a probability tree representation of the CS model. There is a probability of  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  S 
                               
                            
                          that the target is sufficiently stored at the time of test; in this case the person assigns the target to the rank position of 1. This probability is conceptualized as the proportion of all the targets, over a group of similar tests, where the targets are sufficiently stored. It is not considered the result of a random drawing from a probability distribution. All the other targets with probability  
                            
                               1 
                               − 
                               
                                  
                                     θ 
                                  
                                  
                                     S 
                                  
                               
                            
                          are insufficiently stored. Nonetheless, insufficient storage can have partial information that enables the person to reject some of the foils as a mismatch. If the partial information can eliminate  
                            b 
                          foils, then there are  
                            
                               n 
                               − 
                               b 
                            
                          viable candidates in a choice set of size  
                            
                               n 
                               − 
                               b 
                            
                         , which is denoted by the conditional probability  
                            
                               
                                  C 
                               
                               
                                  n

                                  b

                               1

                            n 
                          items; this case of non-storage among the insufficiently stored items has a conditional probability of  
                            
                               
                                  C 
                               
                               
                                  n 
                               
                            
                         . The choice set conditional probabilities

                                     2

                               … 
                               ,

                                     n 
                                  
                               
                            
                          are not considered the result of a random stochastic process. Instead the choice set conditional probabilities represent the proportions for the various levels of target knowledge that occurred over all the cases in which there was  insufficient target storage . Consequently, there is no  
                            
                               
                                  C 
                               
                               
                                  1

                            12 
                         
                         
                            12 
                            With the population-parameter mapping method for estimating the parameters of a MPT model, it is possible to estimate the Bayesian posterior-to-prior odds ratio for the coherence of the model, even for saturated models ( Chechile, 2010 ). So, it is possible, depending on the data and the sample size, for there to be evidence against a saturated MPT model. For example if the target were considerably more likely to have a rank of 2 rather than 1, then the posterior-to-prior odds for model coherence would be low, thus indicating that the model is unsuitable for those data.

In light of the above differences between the 2HT and CS models, it is important to examine the hazard function for the CS model.  Table 2  provides two examples for a six-item ranking task.

Table 2  provides the results from two different configurations for the choice set distribution

                               q 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  k 
                                  )

                                  C

                               ≥ 
                               2

                               q 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  k 
                                  )
