                               4 
                               . 
                               901 
                            
                         . Thus fractional storage is more effective in Example 1 than in Example 2 because the size of the choice set on average is smaller.

Kellen and Klauer (2014)  drew attention to the value of the ranking task for providing the type of data that enables a critical test between the SDT model and the 2HT model. Their critical test is dependent on doing a selective influence experiment that increases the  
                            t 
                          parameter of the 2HT model between two conditions but has no effect on the  
                            
                               q 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  1 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               q 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  k 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          parameters. In this paper we also use the ranking task, but instead establish a different critical test for the 2HT model that can be examined in any single experimental condition, thus bypassing the need for doing a selective influence experiment.

We have been able to prove that the 2HT model predicts monotonically increasing hazard for  
                            
                               k 
                               > 
                               1 
                            
                         . This was true for each of three versions of the model. Each proof consisted of three main steps. First, we derived a distribution  
                            
                               Q 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  k 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          of the probability that  
                            k 
                          items (the target and  
                            
                               k 
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                          foils) are in the uncertain state. This differentiated the homogeneous, heterogeneous and U-state competition versions. Second, we showed the  
                            
                               q 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  k 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          distribution, which represents the probability that the target is assigned rank  
                            k 
                         , given it is in the uncertain state, is a SLC sequence. Third, we established by  Theorem 1  that all SLC sequences have a monotonically increasing hazard. This led to a critical test for any model of recognition memory, regardless if it is the 2HT model or some other model; if it predicts that the  
                            
                               q 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  k 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          distribution is SLC, then it also predicts a monotonically increasing hazard function for  
                            
                               k 
                               > 
                               1 
                            
                         . Therefore, if the empirical hazard function reliably exhibits a local minimum for  
                            
                               k 
                               > 
                               2 
                            
                         , then the model should be rejected.

The question arises: can the 2HT model be modified so that it does not result in a SLC sequence for the  
                            
                               q 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  k 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          distribution and thus exhibit monotonically increasing hazard for  
                            
                               k 
                               > 
                               1 
                            
                         ? We do not believe so. To be a threshold model in the first place, it is a requirement to stipulate the hypothesis that the target has a probability of  
                            t 
                          for being in the state of target detection and has the probability of  
                            
                               1 
                               − 
                               t 
                            
                          for the target being in an uncertain state. However, the assumption that a foil cannot be in the target detection state might be relaxed. Does this modification result in changing the prediction about the hazard for  
                            
                               k 
                               > 
                               1 
                            
                         ? We believe this modification is a special case of the U-state competition model. In the U-state model, each foil has a Bernoulli-process success rate value  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          for having greater strength than the target item. When one or more foils have a  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          value that is very large, say  
                            
                               . 
                               999999 
                            
                         , then it is virtually certain that it is stronger than the target, so that foil will receive a lower rank score than the target. Because it is shown that regardless of the  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          values of the Bernoulli variables of a Poisson-binomial distribution, the resulting distribution is a SLC sequence, so the hazard is increasing for  
                            
                               k 
                               > 
                               1 
                            
                         . Consequently, we believe that the 2HT model must predict monotonically increasing hazard.

So, if monotonically increasing hazard for  
                            
                               k 
                               > 
                               1 
                            
                          is a necessary property of the 2HT model, then how can the CS model, which is very similar to the 2HT model, have a hazard function that is not necessarily monotonically increasing? In the CS model, there is a proportion over all the trials where the target is sufficiently stored. The value for target storage  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  S 
                               
                            
                          is not an outcome from a common stochastic process, but rather it is simply the proportion of targets that are sufficiently stored at the time of test. If  
                            
                               
                                  θ 
                               
                               
                                  S 
                               
                            
                          were a random variable, then a given target on some test trials might be insufficiently stored whereas on an immediate retest of that item it might be sufficiently stored. In the CS model, sufficient storage is a property of the informational content of the target at the time of test. While some targets are sufficiently stored, others are fractionally stored or not stored at all. The CS model thus represents the degree of knowledge of the target as a mixture over all the targets studied. Of the trials when the target is not sufficiently stored, there are  
                            
                               n 
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                          cases where the degree of knowledge of the target results in a choice set of viable candidates. The values for the proportions for these  
                            
                               n 
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                          cases (i.e., the choice set  
                            
                               
                                  C 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          distribution  
                            
                               i 
                               = 
                               2 
                               , 
                               … 
                               , 
                               n 
                            
                          shown in  Fig. 3 ) are also not a random result from a single stochastic process. Each of the  
                            
                               
                                  C 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          values is the proportion over all of the insufficient storage cases where there are  
                            
                               n 
                               − 
                               i 
                            
                          foils that can be eliminated from contention due to the degree of  fractional storage of the target . Each of these  
                            
                               
                                  C 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          values would not fluctuate randomly upon an immediate retest. Thus these values and the resulting values for  
                            
                               q 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  k 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          from  (37)  are not necessarily a SLC sequence. Consequently, this seemingly similar model to the 2HT model is different in several important ways. In the CS model there is not a single uncertain state for non-stored targets with a common stochastic probability distribution for the various outcomes; instead there are  
                            
                               n 
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                          uncertain states depending on the degree of fractional storage of the target.

To have a practical test using the ranking task, three issues need to be addressed. The first concerns whether features of the ranking procedure may affect the outcome; the second concerns the problem of mixtures that might distort the hazard function; and the third concerns the development of an appropriate statistical test. We discuss each issue in turn.

Issues Concerning the Ranking Protocols

The first issue deals with the topic of adequate experimental control. As with any other experimental procedure, investigators need to be careful about insuring that biases are not introduced with the ranking task. If the test items were displayed, then it would be advisable to arrange the items in a nonlinear structure such as a grid or a circular pattern. Moreover the location of the target should be randomized or counterbalanced over testing replications. Also, foils from prior tests should not be similar to foils used for later tests. In general it is important that the experimental procedure, like the protocols used in standard memory research, should carefully control the properties of the stimuli used for targets and foils and should avoid biases introduced by the testing procedure itself.

It is also important for investigators to be confident that participants can meaningfully do the n-item ranking task. Since  
                            
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  n 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               1 
                            
                          for all discrete distributions, a four-item list is the smallest number of test items to assess the 2HT model by testing if  
                            
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  2 
                                  ) 
                               
                               > 
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  3 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                         . If  
                            n 
                          were greater than four, there would be more rank positions to examine for a local hazard minimum. Also goodness-of-fit comparisons among rival theories have a greater chance to ascertain differences if the hazard function has more points. 
                            13 
                         
                         
                            13 
                            It should be pointed out that the number of points for the hazard function is one more than the number of order judgments. For example, with a four item list there are three order judgments and four ranked items. It is theoretically possible to have an experiment with say eight items that only requires three order judgments (i.e., the participants are asked to rank the three most likely items for being the target). But in this case all the non-ranked items have the collective rank of  
                                  4 
                               . So, for this experimental procedure, there cannot be a meaningful eight-point hazard function. Thus, despite having eight items, there is only a four-position hazard function. 
                          Yet there is an experimental-design difficulty in creating a pool of unrelated foils when  
                            n 
                          gets large. A well controlled experiment, where there are  
                            m 
                          test replications per participant, requires the generation of  
                            
                               m 
                               × 
                               n 
                            
                          homogeneous stimuli from which to select targets and foils. When the total pool of stimuli is large, it is challenging to select foils that can be arranged over the test replications such that the succeeding foils are unrelated to any previously presented items. But regardless of the  
                            n 
                          value chosen, there are two related methodological questions: can participants do this task, and what value for  
                            n 
                          is feasible? Clearly instructions are important to overcome ranking reluctance and to inform participants that they are to rank each item in terms of the perceived likelihood that the item was the target. Yet there is a simple test to reassure investigators that they were successful with their instructions and their choice of  
                            n 
                         . The data for the ranking task are frequency values  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               
                               … 
                               , 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                          for the target being in the  
                            n 
                          rank positions (e.g.,  
                            
                               
                                  f 
                               
                               
                                  k 
                               
                            
                          is the number of trials when the target is ranked in position  
                            k 
                         ). If participants are ranking items in terms of the perceived likelihood of the item being the target, then between rank positions  
                            k 
                          and  
                            
                               k 
                               + 
                               1 
                            
                         , we should expect to find that  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     k 
                                  
                               
                               > 
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     k 
                                     + 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                            
                         . If this expected inequality does not hold for the overall data, then the investigator has evidence that the procedure ( including the choice for  
                         
                            n 
                          
                         and the instructions ) has not succeeded, and a modified design is required. Although this inequality test of adjacent frequencies can validate that the participants understood and performed the ranking task, the test itself depends on a sufficiently large value for  
                            
                               N 
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     ∑ 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     i 
                                  
                               
                            
                          for detecting potentially small frequency differences.

As with other experimental tasks, researchers might need to explore their testing protocols in order to be confident about ranking judgments. It also would be reasonable for investigators to use a variety of methods in a search for a procedure that is practical and valid. For example, instead of having participants directly assign numerical ranking values to the test stimuli, it might be useful to use a series of contingent forced-choice recognition tests. With this alternative method, the participant is given a forced-choice test with  
                            n 
                          items (one target and  
                            
                               n 
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                          foils). The participant’s task is to choose the target. If the person is correct, then that corresponds to a rank position of  
                            1 
                         , and the testing for that set of items is completed. But if the participant is incorrect, then the wrong item is removed from the list of test items, and the person is asked to choose again from the  
                            
                               n 
                               − 
                               1 
                            
                          remaining items. If the participant is correct on the first followup forced-choice test, then the target receives the rank value of  
                            2 
                         , and the testing for the set of items is completed. Testing continues in this fashion until the participant is correct or until the person has been tested on a final two-alternative test. If the participant is correct when there is a lineup of  
                            
                               n 
                               − 
                               m 
                            
                          items, then the rank for the target is  
                            
                               m 
                               + 
                               1 
                            
                         . But if the person is wrong on a final two-alternative test, then the target has a rank of  
                            n 
                         . This alternative testing procedure provides more structure to the ranking task, but at the cost of adding more programming effort for implementing the experimental protocol. One advantage of this procedure is it avoids the unnecessary requirement of obtaining the relative ranking of foils that are not yet chosen, and thereby it might make the testing faster and less frustrating for the participants. For example, if the participant believes that some foils were clearly not the target, then it might be difficult for the participant to rank those stimuli. Consequently the ranking might be easier for participants to perform with this experimental procedure.

Concerns about Distorting Mixtures

Chechile (2003)  has shown that a mixture of distributions, each with a monotonically increasing hazard, may not itself have a monotonically increasing hazard. The conditions under which this occurs depends upon properties of the distributions and the mixing rate ( Chechile, 2003, 2013 ). The general finding for two-component mixtures is that the mixture hazard function continues to be monotonically increasing, provided that the separation between the two components is less than a critical distance. This general finding has been demonstrated for Gaussian, Gumbel, and logistic distributions. Moreover, the critical separation distance depends on the mixing rate between the two components. For example, in the Gaussian case with unit variance for each component, the critical separation is 2.70 for the mixing rate of  
                            
                               50 
                            
                          percent for each component; whereas if the mixing rate is  
                            
                               95 
                            
                          percent for the upper component and  
                            5 
                          percent for the lower component, then the critical separation is 3.61 ( Chechile, 2013 ). These theoretical explorations suggest that mixtures of components must be very different before the hazard function exhibits a phase shift from being monotonically increasing to a function with a local minimum at a value other than the lower limit for the support for the random variable.

To check that the above remarks about mixtures of continuous distributions also generally apply for discrete probability distributions, several examples were studied for the homogeneous version of the 2HT model. For each case,  
                            
                               n 
                               = 
                               6 
                            
                          and the mixture consisted of two components where  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               < 
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                            
                         . When there is an even contribution from the two components and  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               . 
                               2 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               . 
                               8 
                            
                         , the resulting values for  
                            
                               q 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  k 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                         ,  
                            
                               k 
                               = 
                               1 
                               , 
                               … 
                               , 
                               6 
                            
                         , are respectively  
                            
                               . 
                               412 
                            
                         ,  
                            
                               . 
                               248 
                            
                         ,  
                            
                               . 
                               144 
                            
                         ,  
                            
                               . 
                               101 
                            
                         ,

                               k 
                               > 
                               1 
                            
                          and has a local minimum at  
                            
                               k 
                               = 
                               3 
                            
                         . As a final example, consider the case where again  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     1

                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     2

                               . 
                               9 
                            
                          but with the lower component contributing  
                            5 
                          percent and the upper component contributing  
                            
                               95 
                            
                          percent. The hazard values for this case are:  
                            
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  2 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               . 
                               226 
                            
                         ,  
                            
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  3 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               . 
                               291 
                            
                         ,  
                            
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  4 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               . 
                               406 
                            
                         , and  
                            
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  5 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               . 
                               617 
                            
                         , and  
                            
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  6 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               1 
                            
                         , which is a monotonically increasing hazard function for  
                            
                               k 
                               > 
                               1 
                            
                         . Consequently, even when there is a very large difference between the two components, the hazard function is still monotonically increasing when there is only a small percentage of the observations from the lower component. These examples demonstrate that a non-monotonic hazard function for a mixture of components, which have individually increasing hazard, requires a very large separation between the components.

The foregoing analyses suggest that if a non-monotonic increasing hazard function is observed, it is necessary to exclude the possibility that this is due to a mixture of sub-groups that have markedly different levels of performance. If this issue seems relevant, then the analysis could be conducted on more homogeneous sub-groups. If non-monotonicity is preserved, then it would be strong evidence against the 2HT model.

Statistical Tests for Hazard

From  Table 1 , which was produced from the SDT framework, a local hazard minimum for  
                            
                               k 
                               > 
                               2 
                            
                          did not occur until  
                            
                               Δ 
                               m 
                               ≥ 
                               2 
                               . 
                               5 
                            
                         . Thus if the SDT model is correct, then to falsify the 2HT model requires a test condition with a high level of target strength. Under those test conditions, it is likely that the modal target ranking is  
                            1 
                         . For example, less than 15 percent of test trials had a rank value greater  
                            1 
                          when  
                            
                               Δ 
                               m 
                               ≥ 
                               2 
                               . 
                               5 
                            
                         . Moreover when  
                            
                               Δ 
                               m 
                               = 
                               3 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           σ 
                                        
                                        
                                           t 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           σ 
                                        
                                        
                                           f 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               1 
                               . 
                               2 
                            
                         , the difference  
                            
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  2 
                                  ) 
                               
                               − 
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  3 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          is only  
                            
                               . 
                               047 
                            
                         . Consequently to obtain statistically reliable hazard values for  
                            
                               k 
                               ≥ 
                               2 
                            
                          will require an experiment with a fairly large sample size  
                            N 
                         . The development of a comprehensive statistical test to assess the change in hazard and the investigation of an appropriate sample size to achieve a desired goal for inferential precision are topics that are beyond the scope of the current paper. But since the data from a ranking task are multinomial frequencies  
                            
                               ( 
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               , 
                               
                               … 
                               , 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                  
                               
                               ) 
                            
                         , there are well developed Bayesian sampling techniques for statistical analyses of multinomial data (e.g.,  Chechile, 2020 ). Monte Carlo methods can thus be employed to address the aforementioned statistical topics. See  Chechile and Pintea (2021)  for an example of how such analyses are done. 
                            14 
                         
                         
                            14 
                            For an alternative frequentist approach, which is based on bootstrap tests, see  Kalish, Dunn, Burdakov, and Sysoev (2016) . 
                          Using these methods to estimate the width of the 95-percent Bayesian posterior interval for  
                            
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  2 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               h

                                  3

                               8

                               . 
                               2

                               007 
                            
                          when  
                            
                               N 
                               ≥ 
                               4000 
                            
                         . In this case the difference between  
                            
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  2 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          and  
                            
                               h 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  3 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          that was mentioned above to show a local hazard minimum for  
                            
                               k 
                               > 
                               2 
                            
                          would be detected as a statistically reliable difference. Although this approximate sample size estimate might seem impractical, it is achievable by pooling data over participants and test trials (e.g., if 100 or more participants are tested on 40 or more test trials).

Finally it is important to remember that the hazard function is a very sensitive method for comparing highly similar models. Consequently after a memory model has been fitted to the data, a comparison between the theoretical and empirical hazard values has the potential for a stringent contrast in the fit quality of the models. When the empirical hazard for large  
                            N 
                          studies have sharply defined interval estimates, such as the 95-percent intervals mentioned above, then it may be challenging for any misspecified model to fit the empirical hazard values. But it is important to note that the models discussed in this paper are not equal in their ease and feasibility for estimation. Parameter estimation is practicable for the SDT, CS, and the homogeneous version of the 2HT model. But the heterogeneous and U-state competition versions of the 2HT model are technically challenging. The problem for both of these cases is due to the estimation difficulties associated with the Poisson-binomial distribution. Given values for the  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          Bernoulli variates, the likelihoods  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     P 
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                     − 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  k 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                          for the Poisson-binomial can be computed, but the inverse problem of extracting estimates of the  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          parameters given only the likelihood values is a mathematically formidable task ( Wang, 1993 ). Yet we can show that the Poisson-binomial distribution is identifiable; see the Appendix for a proof about the identifiability of this probability model. It thus follows that there exists a solution to the inverse problem. But the mathematical complexity of the estimation process explodes rapidly with the size of  
                            n 
                         . Even for relatively small values for  
                            n 
                         , it is difficult to fit the model because it requires solving a thorny set of nonlinear equations. Nonetheless it was still possible in this paper to discover properties of the Poisson-binomial by way of formal proofs. This point underscores the value of mathematical analyses for understanding stochastic processes.

The authors wish to thank Kym McCormick whose astute observation initiated our focus on the hazard function of ranking task. The authors also wish to thank David Kellen and Richard Shiffrin for providing a number of valuable comments and suggestions.

When two or more different configurations of model parameters have values that produce the same set of likelihoods, these configurations are said to be  observationally equivalent  ( Chechile, 1977; Drèze, 1975 ). A stochastic process is identifiable if there are no cases of multiple points in the model space that are observationally equivalent. But for the Poisson-binomial distribution, it is important to distinguish between a standardized and non-standardized form of the  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          values for  
                            
                               i 
                               = 
                               1 
                               , 
                               … 
                               , 
                               n 
                            
                         . For example, consider the following case where  
                            
                               n 
                               = 
                               2 
                            
                         . It is trivially true that the combination of  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               . 
                               1 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               . 
                               7 
                            
                          will produce the same likelihoods as the combination  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               . 
                               7 
                            
                          and  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               . 
                               1 
                            
                         . So, the Poisson-binomial distribution is not identifiable when the Bernoulli variates are discriminable (i.e., if we are interested in specific Bernoulli variables and their corresponding  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          values). Yet in many applications, we only care about the set of  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          values without being concerned about the linkage between specific Bernoulli variates and their corresponding  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          values. For these applications, the Bernoulli variates can be arranged in a standardized form where  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               ≤ 
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               ≤ 
                               ⋯ 
                               ≤ 
                               
                                  
                                     p 
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                  
                               
                            
                         , and we make no claims as to which specific Bernoulli variable has which  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          value. In this case the issue about identifiability of the Poisson binomial is about whether or not there are multiple configurations of the  
                            
                               
                                  p 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                          values in standardized form that are observationally equivalent. Note for the above example of two configurations for  
                            
                               n 
                               = 
                               2 
                            
                         , which are observationally equivalent, that these configurations are actually the same configuration in standardized form. Thus the critical question for the Poisson-binomial concerns whether or not there are different points in standardized form that are observationally equivalent.

Theorem 6 
                            
                               For the Poisson-binomial distribution for  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           p 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     … 
                                     , 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           p 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                
                               Bernoulli indicators, there are no cases of different  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               configurations in standardized form that are observationally equivalent.

For the Poisson-binomial distribution for  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           p 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     … 
                                     , 
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           p 
                                        
                                        
                                           n 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                
                               Bernoulli indicators, there are no cases of different  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        p 
                                     
                                     
                                        i 
                                     
                                  
                                
                               configurations in standardized form that are observationally equivalent.

Proof

                                        p

                                                    1 
                                                    −

                                              ,

                                                    i 
                                                    = 
                                                    1 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    n 
                                                 
                                              
                                              
                                                 
                                                    
                                                       
                                                          p 
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          i 
                                                       
                                                    
                                                    + 
                                                    
                                                       
                                                          ε 
                                                       
                                                       
                                                          i 
                                                       
                                                    
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    1 
                                                    − 
                                                    
                                                       
                                                          p

One solution is the case where  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           ε 
                                        
                                        
                                           i 
                                        
                                     
                                     = 
                                     0

                                  i

                                           i

                                  i 
                               , which means that the two points are equivalent. So, this case is not a demonstration of different points that are observationally equivalent. The second possible solution is the case where

                                     =

                                for all

                                           n

                               . Consequently, there cannot be observationally equivalent model-space points in standardized form. □

Let us begin a proof by contradiction with the assumption that there exist two points in standardized form that have the same set of likelihoods for the Poisson-binomial distribution. Let us denote the other point as
